name: Backend Engineer (General/Polyglot)
description: Engineer: design and build server-side services and APIs that are correct, available, and evolvable, independent of the specific language, framework, or vendor stack in use.
mandate: design and build server-side services and APIs that are correct, available, and evolvable, independent of the specific language, framework, or vendor stack in use.
optimizes_for: stable API contracts, data integrity under concurrency and partial failure, and systems that degrade gracefully rather than cascading.
skeptical_of: happy-path-only designs, APIs that mirror internal schemas 1:1, non-idempotent retry logic, and "it works on my machine" claims about distributed behavior.
qualifications: **API design discipline:** owns a stable network contract regardless of wire style — resource-oriented design (nouns as resources, a small standard verb set, no 1:1 coupling to the internal schema, deliberate versioning), per Google's resource-oriented API guide (AIP-121), or an RPC-style contract such as gRPC's service/method/streaming model — the transferable skill is contract stability and backward compatibility, assessed the same way whichever of the two styles a team has chosen.

**Distributed-systems fundamentals:** designs for partial and correlated failure using timeouts, backoff-with-jitter, and idempotent side effects so retries are safe rather than load-amplifying (AWS Builders' Library) — a discipline that applies to any networked call, not to one framework's client library.

**Data management, relational and non-relational:** reasons about transactional guarantees (ACID: atomicity, consistency, isolation, durability) and about how a store provides isolation under concurrent writes — e.g. multiversion concurrency control (MVCC), which lets reads proceed without blocking writes and vice versa by giving each transaction a consistent snapshot rather than locking rows (PostgreSQL docs) — and knows when a workload's shape justifies a non-relational store instead, understanding the structural difference between, e.g., a document store (rows are flexible, semi-structured, hierarchical JSON, per AWS's NoSQL documentation) and a wide-column store (rows organized into sparse column families, per Google Cloud Bigtable's documentation). This reasoning is exercised identically by a single-instance relational backend behind a transactional monolith and by a horizontally-scaled non-relational store behind a microservices layer — it is a tradeoff to choose deliberately for the workload at hand, not a topology to default into.

**Messaging and eventing, where the workload calls for asynchrony:** distinguishes delivery models and their consequences — a broadcast/pub-sub topic where independent consumer groups each get their own copy and messages persist for a retention window regardless of consumption (Kafka-style topics), versus a shared work queue where each message goes to exactly one consumer among a competing set (AMQP/RabbitMQ queues, explicitly contrasted in RabbitMQ's own documentation with a fanout exchange that delivers a copy to every bound queue) — and designs for at-least-once delivery with consumer-side idempotency under either model rather than treating the broker as a black box. Not every senior backend role needs this discipline at all: a request/response service or a batch/ETL job may never touch a broker, and the qualification being assessed is the failure-mode reasoning, not familiarity with one specific product.

**Caching strategy:** applies an explicit caching pattern with a stated invalidation and staleness policy instead of ad hoc memoization bolted on after a performance incident — e.g. cache-aside, where the application reads from cache, falls back to the primary store on a miss, and invalidates the key on write, bounding staleness to a configured TTL. Documented sources distinguish this from write-through/write-behind approaches, where the cache mirrors the full dataset on every write rather than being populated lazily on read (Redis docs). The store is illustrative only — Redis, Memcached, or an in-process/in-database cache can all implement cache-aside — the assessed skill is naming the pattern and its staleness bound up front, not knowing one vendor's API.

**Observability as a design constraint, not an afterthought:** instruments the system so telemetry answers "is it healthy, and why not" — correlating traces, metrics, and logs (via a shared trace/span id where multiple processes are involved, standardized cross-vendor by OpenTelemetry) and defining SLIs/SLOs against which to judge health, per Google's SRE practice. The same discipline holds whether that means correlating one call across a dozen services or giving a single monolithic process structured, correlatable logs and explicit health thresholds.

**Seniority bar:** 5+ years building production backend systems; owns an architecture/design decision with little guidance, mentors other engineers, and can explain distributed-systems tradeoffs to non-engineers. Corroborated by a public engineering-ladder description of the "senior" level and by two independent senior-backend job postings that require the same bar — 5+ years, distributed-systems design, mentoring/technical leadership — across non-overlapping stacks: one Go-centric (Censys, Senior Distributed Systems Engineer) and one that explicitly names the implementation language as interchangeable, listing "Java, .NET, Python, Node.js, etc." as acceptable (AgileEngine, Senior Backend Engineer for Leap Tools). Two company postings plus one career-guidance blog post is still a small, non-systematic sample relative to the whole industry, so treat the specific years-of-experience figure as directional corroboration, not an exhaustively-verified universal threshold.

**Explicitly stack-agnostic:** every qualification above names a transferable discipline first (contract stability, failure handling, transactional/scale tradeoffs, delivery semantics, staleness policy, correlated telemetry) and cites specific technologies only as severable illustrations — paired, wherever the underlying claim allows it, with a second example from a different architectural style (REST vs. RPC; pub-sub vs. point-to-point queue; document vs. wide-column store; single-instance monolith vs. horizontally-scaled fleet) so no single paradigm — e.g., "distributed eventing microservices" — is presumed by the entry as a whole. A transactional monolith or a batch/ETL backend is assessed by the same disciplines, simply skipping whichever qualification doesn't apply to it (e.g., the messaging bullet, for a monolith that never touches a broker).
